coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of thiabendazole in fruits
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Song
Zhu, Haimei
Cai, Shenghao
Chen, Zifan
Liang, Xiao
Li, Zhuo
Peng, Nannan
Yang, Yang
Wang, Jingmin
Wang, Yongzhong
coredata.dc:description: Quantitative determination of pesticides in fruits and vegetables is essential for human healths. Herein, a new dual-emission carbon dots with high fluorescence stability at a pH range of 4–10 and a temperature range of 0–60 °C was synthesized. And a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe was proposed to detect thiabendazole (TBZ) residue with a wide linear range (0–1000 μM) and low detection limit (0.15 μM). The emission at 512 nm exhibited a special “turn-off” fluorescence sensing of TBZ due to internal filter effect, while that at 361 nm barely changed and worked as reference. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescence strategy was successfully applied for determining TBZ in fruits with good recoveries (96.73%–111.17 %, 93.29%–120.78 % and 96.28%–100.57 %, respectively). Notably, the constructed ratiometric fluorescence probe had comparable accuracy to HPLC in detecting unknown concentrations of TBZ in pear juice, demonstrating dual-emission carbon dots possess wide and promising applicability for fluorescence sensing pesticides in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence detection
Internal filter effect
Thiabendazole
Food security

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection specificity of PT-CDs for TBZ
Detection recoveries of PT-CDs for TBZ in real samples
Detection accuracy of PT-CDs for TBZ in real samples
Detection specificity of PT-CDs for TBZ
Detection of PT-CDs for TBZ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Y Z Wang, grant No. 31770066 and 31470218 ), the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang, No. KJ2021A0051 ) and the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University (JM Wang). All co-authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM image and (b) particle size distribution of the PT-CDs. (c) HRTEM images of the PT-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) high-resolution C 1s, (c) high-resolution N 1s and (d) high-resolution O 1s spectra of the PT-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra (λ ex = 330 nm) of PT-CDs solution (Inset: the corresponding photographs under visible light and UV lamp (365 nm), respectively). PL intensity and ratiometric fluorescence of the PT-CDs at (b) different pH (4-10), (c) different temperature (4 °C, 17 °C, 25 °C, 37 °C, 50 °C and 60 °C).

(a) PL intensity and ratiometric fluorescence of the PT-CDs in different fruit ingredients. (b) PL emission spectra of PT-CDs added with various concentration of TBZ. (c) The relationship between F361/F512 and concentration of TBZ. (λ ex = 330 nm, CPT-CDs = 0.05 mg mL−1).

(a) A liner relationship between F361/F512 and concentration of TBZ during three point-in-time one day. (b) A liner relationship between F361/F512 and concentration of TBZ in three consecutive days. (λex = 330 nm, CPT-CDs = 0.05 mg mL−1).

(a) UV–vis spectra of TBZ, PT-CDs in the absence and presence of TBZ, the sum curve of PT-CDs and TBZ, fluorescent emission spectra (green dotted line) of PT-CDs. (b) Fluorescence lifetime experiments of PT-CDs in the absence and presence of TBZ.
Schematic illustration of dual-colored PT-CDs based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for TBZ detection.
PT-CDs for ratiometric fluoreTBZand cucumber samples with standard addition method (n = 3).
Comparison of results between PT-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence (Ratio FL) and HPLC methods for detecting TBZ in pear (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of thiabendazole in fruits

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, Anhui, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantitative determination of pesticides in fruits and vegetables is essential for human healths. Herein, a new dual-emission carbon dots with high fluorescence stability at a pH range of 4–10 and a temperature range of 0–60 °C was synthesized. And a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe was proposed to detect thiabendazole (TBZ) residue with a wide linear range (0–1000 μM) and low detection limit (0.15 μM). The emission at 512 nm exhibited a special “turn-off” fluorescence sensing of TBZ due to internal filter effect, while that at 361 nm barely changed and worked as reference. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescence strategy was successfully applied for determining TBZ in fruits with good recoveries (96.73%–111.17 %, 93.29%–120.78 % and 96.28%–100.57 %, respectively). Notably, the constructed ratiometric fluorescence probe had comparable accuracy to HPLC in detecting unknown concentrations of TBZ in pear juice, demonstrating dual-emission carbon dots possess wide and promising applicability for fluorescence sensing pesticides in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of social economy and the continuous improvement of people's living standards, food safety issues have gradually become the focus of attention [1–3]. According to food contamination monitoring statistics, the main cause of these issues is pesticide and veterinary drug residues, accounting for 51.28 % of the problem sources, with residues in edible agricultural products being much higher than those in other foods. The fruit and vegetable industry, as one of the important pillar industries in many countries, the quality and safety occupy a very important role in the economic development, directly affecting the industry's credibility and interests worldwide [1–3]. Currently, the main safety hazards in fruit and vegetable industry come from massive application of chemical pesticides, especially benzimidazoles (BMZs) pesticides. BMZs, including carbendazim (MBC), thiophanate-methyl (TM), thiabendazole (TBZ), and benzendazim, can effectively inhibit the reproduction and growth of pathogenic bacteria by forming hydrogen bonds with enzymes and receptors in organisms, as well as coordinating with metal ions, or undergoing reactions such as hydrophobic-hydrophobic and π-π interactions [4]. However, BMZs are susceptible to endosorption by agricultural products, causing the food contamination. Meanwhile, long-term consumption of such products may endanger human health, such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, fetal malformation/premature birth, and other diseases [5,6]. Worse still, some BMZs, such as TBZ, are also prone to environmental contamination and even other serious consequences (e.g., enhancing resistance of pathogens and pests, harming beneficial organisms, wildlife, and livestock poisoning) due to its non-degradable defects, ultimately leading to the emergence of harmful products and unsafe foods, posing significant potential risks to human health. To ensure the food safety, China has established standards “GB2763-2021” that specify the maximum residue limits (MRL) for TBZ in pears, apples, oranges and bananas is 15 mg/kg [7]. Thus, there is an indispensable to develop effective TBZ detection approaches in fruits and vegetables to ensure food safety. So far, conventional analysis methods, such as gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS) have been widely used in the pesticide determination of ISO 17025 accreditation standards due to their high accuracy, reproducibility and high detection accuracy, providing support for environmental quality and food safety monitoring [8,9]. However, the practical application of these methods is limited by cumbersome pre-treatment, complicated operations, time-consuming testing, high analytical costs and high requirements for instrument operators, particularly emergency cases. Considering the limitations of traditional methods, there is an urgent need to establish direct and reliable sensing technology for pesticide detection. Fluorescent sensors based on nanomaterials have gradually become a candidate for pesticides analysis because of their simplicity, speediness, high sensitivity and selectivity [8–10].

With the development of advanced functional materials, carbon nanomaterials such as graphene, fullerene C60, carbon nanotubes and organic frameworks materials provide a new way for the establishment of fluorescence sensing strategies [11–13]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, endow the advantage of low toxicity, photobleaching resistance, good biocompatibility, easy surface functionalization, wide sources, low expense and easy synthesis, presenting enormous application potential in fields of catalysis, chemo/biosensing, solar cells, bioimaging and biotherapy [14–17]. Although CDs act as one of the irreplaceable materials in fluorescence detecting various metal ions, antibiotics, food additives, pesticides and biomolecule [8,9,18], the majority of CDs reported so far are single wavelengths and the analysis results are susceptible to interference from various factors such as environment, light source, probe concentration and instrumentation [19,20]. To overcome these drawbacks and achieve precise detection of complex systems, the ratiometric fluorescence technology has attracted widespread attention, which can reduce the interference of food substrates on fluorescence signals through signal separation and signal overlap, and avoid the influence of various external factors on detection results [21–23]. Currently, the design of CDs based ratiometric fluorescence platforms mainly adopts the following categories: CDs hybridized with fluorescence materials (such as quantum dots [24,25], metal nanoclusters [26,27] and others [28,29]) into dual-emission nanomaterials. However, the high cost of such hybrid precursors and the potential environmental, food and human hazards result from the introduction have restricted their further application. The second approach is intersystem crossing induced by transition metal ions (Cu2+, Gd3+, etc.) doped CDs [30,31]. Nevertheless, the response fluorescence come from intersystem crossing always close to the CDs’ fluorescence, leading to significant interference between the two signals and reduced sensitivity of the probes. Besides, the potential pollution caused by metal doping cannot be ignored. Mixing different fluorescent CDs is the third method [32,33], which may result in the shift or even quenching of fluorescence peaks, increasing the difficulty of probe construction. Fortunately, the emergence of dual-emission CDs brings new hope to the above-mentioned thorny issues. It not only overcomes all the above-mentioned shortcomings, but also maximizes the optical performance of CDs and simplifies the complex process of constructing ratiometric fluorescence probes [10,34–37]. Up to now, the dual-emission CDs have realized the ratiometric fluorescence detection of nitrite [10], veterinary drugs [34], acid red 18 [35], morphine [36] and propafenone hydrochloride [37], but the detection of TBZ residues in fruits and vegetables is rarely reported. Therefore, it is meaningful to establish dual-emission CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensors for selectively and sensitively detecting TBZ.

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emission CDs (PT-CDs) was constructed for sensitive determination of TBZ (Scheme 1 ). The PT-CDs exhibited two emission peaks centered at 361 and 512 nm under excitation of 330 nm, which maintained stable at pH 4–10 and temperature 0–60 °C. As the TBZ concentration increased from 0 to 1000 μM, the fluorescence peak intensity at 512 nm gradually decreased, while that at 361 nm remained basically unchanged. More importantly, PT-CDs based ratiometric sensing of TBZ was successfully achieved in fruits (pear, apple and cucumber) with good recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) by standard addition method. Furthermore, it was demonstrated that the probe has comparable sensitivity and accuracy to HPLC by comparing detection results of TBZ in pear juice. Consequently, this work provides a highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-emission CDs for pesticide residues and ensure a powerful and efficient tool in the monitoring of the quality and safety of food.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. o-phenylenediamine, tryptophan, sucrose, glucose, laevulose, cysteine, methionine, glutamic acid, glycine, alanine, tartaric acid, vitamin C, MgSO4, KCl, NaCl, benzendazim, carbendazim, thiabendazole were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Solution of various pH values were prepared with different ratios between NaOH and HCl. The fruits were purchased from the convenience store on the Anhui University campus.

The morphology of samples were obtained by the transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). The structural of samples were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), where FTIR spectroscopy analyses were tested using a Vertex80+Hyperion2000 spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) employing the KBr pellet method and XPS spectra were performed by Thermo Electron ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (New York, America). The Techcomp FL970 plus fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) and Horiba Fluoro max plus ultrafast time resolved fluorescence spectrometer were used to examine fluorescence and fluorescence lifetime of samples, respectively. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a MAPADA UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Nexera UHPLC/HPLC System Ultrafast liquid chromatograph (Shanghai, China) was used to detect TBZ content in actual samples to compare the results of fluorescence detection.

As illustrated in Scheme 1, PT-CDs was prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 4 g o-phenylenediamine and 4 g tryptophan were added to 40 mL of ddH2O by ultra-sonication. Then placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, followed by heating at 180 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the products were centrifuged at 10,300 g for 10 min and filtered with 0.22 μm filtration membrane. To further purify the PT-CDs, the solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (1000 kDa molecular weight cut-off) for 48 h. Finally, the brown solution was dried into powder (PT-CDs) by vacuum freeze drying and preserved for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection specificity of PT-CDs for TBZ
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared PT-CDs solid powder was dissolved in DMSO (5 mg mL−1) for the fluorescence measurements. Sucrose, glucose, laevulose, cysteine, methionine, glutamic acid, glycine, alanine, tartaric acid, vitamin C, MgSO4, KCl, NaCl, benzendazim, carbendazim, thiabendazole were configured as 1 mM in DMSO to investigate the selectivity of PT-CDs on TBZ. In detail, 30 μL of PT-CDs standard solution and 2970 μL of the above solution were added into the Colorimetric dish (volume of 3 mL) and incubated for 5 min. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded excited by 330 nm to determine F361, F512 and F361/F512 (F361 and F512 were the fluorescence intensities of PT-CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection recoveries of PT-CDs for TBZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The pre-processing steps including: pear, apple and cucumber were weighed, cut into pieces, juiced separately by a blender and filtered through four layers of gauze into clean beakers, respectively. The juice was then dispensed into several 15 mL EP tubes, centrifuged at 6500 g for 5 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous filtration membrane, dispensed, labeled and refrigerated at 4 °C.

Standard solutions of TBZ at concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 100 μM were configured, and the standard curves of the assay were plotted using the fluorescence method, in which 0.05 mg mL−1 PT-CDs act as ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSD) of the ratiometric fluorescence method in real samples were obtained by adding TBZ solutions of known concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μM) to each juice using the standard addition method, and then detecting the emission spectra of the solutions, calculating the concentrations according to the standard curves, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection accuracy of PT-CDs for TBZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Pears were weighed and chopped, divided into two portions and soaked for 4 h in equal volumes of blank solvent and high concentration of TBZ solution, respectively. The soaked pear pieces were juiced separately, filtered through gauze, centrifuged (6500 g, 5 min), and the supernatant was obtained by filtration through 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane, labeled separately (solvent group, TBZ residue group), and refrigerated at 4 °C.

HPLC detection method: The chromatographic column was a WondaSil-C18 Superb 5 μm column (4.6 × 250 mm), the mobile phase was methanol-water (70:30, v/v), the flow rate was 1.0 mL/min, the detection wavelength was 300 nm, the injection volume was 10 μL, and the column temperature was 40 °C. Chromatographic grade methanol, double-distilled water, standard solution, solvent DMSO over 0.45 μm filter membrane, sonication for 30 min to remove air bubbles. The TBZ standard solutions (concentrations of 0, 10, 30, 40, 50 and 100 μM) were configured and tested to obtain the standard curve, using the above blank juice set as solvent. The TBZ residue solutions group were then measured at the same conditions. Finally, the concentration of TBZ residue in pear was calculated from the standard curve.

Fluorescence detection method: The standard curve was plotted using the same method as in 2.5, except that the solvent was replaced with the solvent solution group. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of the samples to be examined were tested in the same way as in 2.5, where the samples were the TBZ residue solutions group of unknown concentration. Finally, the residual concentration of TBZ was calculated from the standard curve.

As depicted in Scheme 1, PT-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method from o-phenylenediamine and tryptophan. And then, the morphologies of PT-CDs were characterized by TEM and HRTEM in Fig. 1 . It can be seen clearly from Fig. 1a that the as-prepared PT-CDs were in a well monodispersed dispersion, quasi-spherical in shape, and uniform in size with an average diameter of 7.25 nm, showing a Gaussian distribution within 3–11 nm (Fig. 1b). From the HRTEM image of PT-CDs in Fig. 1c, a carbon lattice spacing of 0.205 nm was observed, which may resemble the (100) graphite facet [7,38]. Moreover, the carbon lattice spacing of 0.336 nm can match with inter-layer distance of graphite [39].

Further, the chemical constituents and functional groups of the obtained PT-CDs were performed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the survey XPS spectrum possessed three characteristic peaks centered at 285, 398 and 531.1 eV, which were observed in the C 1s, N 1s and O 1s with the corresponding atomic concentrations of 78.97 %, 20.27 % and 5.75 %, respectively. The high resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 2b) displayed three different peaks, illustrating the existence of three different carbon chemical states in PT-CDs. The peak at 284.5 eV can be assigned to the C=C/C–C of PT-CDs [20,36]. The peak at 285.8 eV can be attributed to the C–O/C–N, while the peak at 289.2 eV can be ascribed to O–C=O [40,41]. The high resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. 2c presented two peaks at 399.5 and 401.2 eV, which were associated with the N–H and C–N–C, respectively [35]. Meanwhile, the high resolution O 1s spectra comprised two peaks at 531.6 and 532.8 eV, belonging to C=O and C–OH/-COOH bands, respectively [20,42]. Subsequently, FT-IR was also investigated to analysis the surface functional groups of PT-CDs. As exhibited in Fig. S1, the presence of N–H and O–H bonds was confirmed by the absorption peaks centered at 3403 and 3290 cm−1, respectively [37,43]. The absorption bands at 2500-3000 cm−1 suggested the presence of C–H [10,44], while the peaks at 1677 and 1632 cm−1 were ascribed to the C=N stretching vibration [45]. Meanwhile, the peaks in the range of 1000–1250 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–N bonds [46], and the absorption peak at 744 cm−1 may correspond to the stretching vibration of the benzene ring. Therefore, the results of FT-IR spectrum corresponding to the presence of the consistent groups as indicated by XPS analysis, confirming that the PT-CDs were composed of sp3 carbon defects, sp2 N-heterocyclic rings and bear hydrophilic groups (such as –NH2 and –OH) on the surface.

As seen in the inset of Fig. 3 a, the as-synthesized PT-CDs present good dispersion and optical properties, so that the solution remained transparent and pale yellow under visible light, while emerged bright blue green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. To explore the optical characteristics of PT-CDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the PT-CDs were recorded by UV/vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrofluorometer, respectively. It can be observed from Fig. 3a that there is an obvious absorption peak at around 263 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, which may be assigned to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 bond [34,47]. And the noticeable peak centered at 422 nm could be ascribed to the n → π* transition of C=O and C=N bonds, which is consistent with the results of Fig. 2 [31,48]. Additionally, the PL spectrum results in Fig. 3a indicated that the blue green light (as shown in the inset) is not monochromatic. On the contrary, it consists of two colours, displaying blue fluorescence peak at 361 nm and green fluorescence peak at 512 nm with excitation of 330 nm.

Besides, the fluorescence emission spectra of PT-CDs at different excitation wavelengths were performed in Fig. S2a, in which the peaks at 361 nm had no obvious change pattern under varied λ ex. On the contrary, the intensity of the peaks at 512 nm gradually increased with the excitation shift from 280 to 340 nm, showing a certain excitation-dependence property. Considering that further exploration of the properties and applications of PT-CDs, it is essential to choose an excitation wavelength that can make both emission peaks have strong intensity, 330 nm was selected in the subsequent studies. Meanwhile, as represented in Fig. S2b, the concentration dependence of the PT-CDs were tested, the results proved that both peaks had strong fluorescence intensity when the concentration of PT-CDs was 0.05 mg mL−1. Therefore, the concentration for the subsequent experiments were all 0.05 mg mL−1.

Moreover, the fluorescence stability of PT-CDs was investigated at different pH and temperature. As shown in Fig. 3b, as pH varied from 4 to 10, there was a negligible fluctuation in the intensity of the peaks at 361 nm, while that at 512 nm remained almost no change. Correspondingly, the line graph was the ratiometric fluorescence of two peaks, reflecting the fluorescence stability of PT-CDs in the pH range of 4–10. To explore the effect of temperature on the fluorescence of PT-CDs, low temperature (4 °C), room temperature (17, 25, 37 °C) and high temperature (50, 60 °C) were selected as the different environment temperature. It was found from Fig. 3c that the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm of PT-CDs and the ratiometric fluorescence of two peaks (line graph) were rarely changed at different temperatures, except for a slight enhancement of the peak intensity at 361 nm with increasing temperature (probably due to particle sizes and the diversity of surface emission sites affected by the temperature [35,49]), indicating that PT-CDs can be used in special environments and at high temperatures throughout the year. Thus, the PT-CDs have good stability in a variety of complex environments (pH 4–10, Temperature 4–60 °C), which lays the foundation for detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection specificity of PT-CDs for TBZ
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the detection specificity of PT-CDs for TBZ, the PL emission spectra of the PT-CDs solutions containing different fruit ingredients, such as sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose), amino acids (cysteine, methionine, glutamic acid, glycine and alanine), others (tartaric acid, vitamin C, Na+, K+, Mg2+) and benzimidazole pesticides (benzendazim, carbendazim and thiabendazole) were recorded. As present in Fig. 4 a, compared to the PT-CDs system with TBZ, the presence of other fruit components had negligible effect on the intensity of the two fluorescence emission peaks of PT-CDs and the value of F361/F512, confirming that the potential of PT-CDs as a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for selective detecting TBZ.

The sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence sensor was evaluated by testing the fluorescence spectra of the PT-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of TBZ. The fluorescence response results were performed in Fig. 4b and Fig. S3, fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged at 361 nm and rapidly decreased at 512 nm with the increasing concentration of TBZ. Accordingly, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F361/F512) was continuously increased with the concentration increased from 0 to 1000 μM. The calibration curve between the F361/F512 versus the concentration of TBZ in a range of 0–1000 μM exhibited a good linear relationship with a coefficient of determination of 0.9976 (Fig. 4c). The linear regression equation was fitted with the Stern-Volmer equation, which can be expressed as y = 0.00219 x +1.51301 (where y was the ratiometric fluorescence intensity, x was TBZ concentration). Meanwhile, the studendized residual plot and response factor plot have been added in the revised manuscript to illustrate the reliability of the linearity based on R2. As shown in Fig. S4a, the residual distribution is basically normal and centered on zero, indicating that the selected function fits the data well [50]. However, positive and negative deviations from linearity are usually found at low and high concentration levels, respectively (Fig. S4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.15 μM based on 3σ/S (where σ was the standard deviation of the intercept, and S was the slope of the linear calibration plot). As per the current Chinese standard “GB2763-2021”, the maximum residual limit for TBZ (15.0 mg/kg or 74 μmol/L) [7], which is much higher than that in our work.

Meanwhile, to determine the intraday (within-day) reliability, the PL emission spectra of PT-CDs added with various concentration of TBZ was measured once in the morning (T1) and approximately three (T2), 6 h (T3) later on the same day, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the PT-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence probe showed a strong intraday reliability. The calibration curve between the F361/F512 versus the concentration of TBZ in a range of 0–1000 μM during three point-in-time one day exhibited a good linear relationship with a coefficient of determination of 0.98072 (Fig. S5a). Similarly, the PL emission spectra of PT-CDs added with various concentration of TBZ was measured for three consecutive days in order to determine the reliability interday (Fig. 5b). The linear regression equation was fitted with the Stern-Volmer equation, which can be expressed as y = 0.00352 x +1.63943 (where y was the ratiometric fluorescence intensity, x was TBZ concentration). And the coefficient of determination was 0.99913 (Fig. S5b). The above results proved that the PT-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence probe has good responsiveness, sensitivity, repeatabilities, and low detection limit for TBZ, making it a promising new type of sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PT-CDs for TBZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by these desirable properties, the practical application of the proposed TBZ quantitative detection methods in fruits was investigated using pear, apple and cucumber as real samples. The recovery experiment was further conducted by spiking different concentration of TBZ into the fruits sample. The calibration curve between the F361/F512 values and the TBZ concentration in fruits were shown in Fig. S6. As illustrated in Table 1 , the recoveries of pear, apple and cucumber samples ranged from 96.73 % to 111.17 %, 93.29 % to 120 0.78 % and 96.28 %–100.57 %, respectively. Moreover, the relative standard deviations (RSD) of the analytical samples were lower than 7.64 % (apple), which confirmed the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method to detect TBZ in real samples.

Furthermore, the pear samples with good detection results were selected to construct detection model by immersing unknown concentrations of TBZ. The detection accuracy of the PT-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor was evaluated by comparing the detection results of fluorescence and HPLC methods. First, the corresponding detection values of two methods were measured and the standard curves were fitted by adding different concentrations of TBZ in pear juice (Fig. S7). Then, the signal values of the constructed detection model (pear juice with unknown TBZ concentration) were detected by ratiometric fluorescence and HPLC methods, respectively, and substituted into the corresponding standard curve in Fig. S7 for calculation. Finally, the calculated TBZ concentrations in the detection model were shown in Table 2 . Although the RSD value of the ratiometric fluorescence detection method was slightly higher than that of HPLC, the average value of its three sets of detection results was very close to that of HPLC, demonstrating that the PT-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor has comparable accuracy to HPLC.

The main mechanisms that cause fluorescence quenching of sensors can generally be divided into: Light induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and internal filter effect (IFE) and static quenching (SQE) or dynamic quenching (DQE) [47,51]. As illustrated in Figs. 4b and 6 a, the PET and FRET were excluded for fluorescence quenching of PT-CDs [52]. The TBZ detection mechanism of IFE, SQE or DQE were then explored through analysis of UV–vis absorption spectra, PL and fluorescence lifetime. It can be observed from Fig. 6a that the strong absorption of TBZ at 300–340 nm limited the excitation energy absorption of PT-CDs at 330 nm, indicating that IFE may promote fluorescence quenching. To further verify the quenching mechanism of IFE, the following equation were calculated [53].
(1)
I
c o r r e c t
I
=
2.3 × d ×
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
×
2.3 × s ×
A
e m
×
10
g
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m
where I, I
correct are the measured maximum fluorescence intensity at 512 nm and the corrected fluorescence intensity removed IFE from I, respectively. A
ex and A
em are the absorbance values of PT-CDs + TBZ at 330 (excitation wavelength) and 512 nm (emission wavelength), respectively. g, s and d here are 0.4, 0.1 and 1.0 cm, respectively. The calculation results were listed in Table S1, and the quenching rate before (actual measured) and after correction (calculated) were very close, indicating that IFE may the main quenching mechanism.

In addition, SQE or DQE based fluorescence sensing mechanism had also been discussed. DQE is a process in which the excited state fluorophore is non-radiometrically deactivated upon collision with the quencher. The occurrence of SQE results in the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between the quencher and the fluorophore, and the light absorbed by the resulting complex can be immediately returned to the ground state without any photon emission [10,54]. Fig. 4b displayed that the fluorescence intensity of PT-CDs gradually decreased with the increase addition of TBZ, confirming the existence of SQE or DQE. Stern-Volmer equation can be carried out to determine the existence of SQE or DQE:
(2)
(
F 361
/
F 512
)
0
(
F 361
/
F 512
)
1
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
(
R 2
= 0.99746
)
where (F 361/F 512)0 and (F 361/F 512)1 are the ratiometric fluorescence of PT-CDs in the absence and presence of TBZ excited by 330 nm, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of TBZ, and K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [10,54]. The K sv was assigned a value of 2.19 × 103 M−1 here, ranging from 0 to 1000 μM for a linear regression equation (Fig. 4c). Since K sv = K qτ0, where τ0 is 2.925995 ns, so that K q (the double molecular quenching constant) were calculated as 7.5 × 1011 M−1s−1, and the low value of K q suggests that the sensing mechanism may originate from DQE [19]. Furthermore, the lifetimes of PT-CDs and PT-CDs + TBZ (Fig. 6b) were measured to distinguish DQE from SQE [19]. The value of PT-CDs (2.925995 ns) were decreased to 2.862997 ns after the addition of TBZ, proving that the DQE may also be one of the quenching mechanism in PT-CDs based sensors.

In summary, a dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (PT-CDs) with high fluorescent stability in environments (at a pH range of 4–10 and a temperature range of 0–60 °C) was successfully prepared, which can act as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting TBZ. In the presence of TBZ, the emission of PT-CDs centered at 512 nm was decreased dramatically because of the IFE and DQE, while the emission at 361 nm unchanged. Good linearity was observed between the ratiometric fluorescence (F361/F512) of the PT-CDs and the concentration of TBZ from 0 to 1000 μM with a low LOD (0.15 μM). More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was successfully applied to the quantitative detection of TBZ in real fruits, with recoveries of 96.73 %–111.17 % (pear), 93.29 %–120.78 % (apple) and 96.28 %–100.57 % (cucumber), respectively. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence detection method present an accuracy comparable to that of HPLC by determining the unknown concentration of TBZ in pear juice. This ratiometric fluorescent strategy provides a simple, fast and effective path to developing CDs based detection platforms for TBZ in fruits and vegetables with wide application prospects.

Song Yang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Haimei Zhu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Shenghao Cai: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Zifan Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiao Liang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Zhuo Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Nannan Peng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Yang Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jingmin Wang: Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yongzhong Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Y Z Wang, grant No. 31770066 and 31470218), the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang, No. KJ2021A0051) and the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University (JM Wang). All co-authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.
